## Overview
Felix J. Herrmann is a **Professor** in the Schools of **Earth and Atmospheric Sciences** and **Computational Science and Engineering** at Georgia Tech, where he leads a research group focused on algorithmic and software foundations for large-scale seismic imaging and inversion. His work spans theoretical and practical aspects of computational geophysics, with particular emphasis on <u>waveform inversion</u>, <u>seismic data reconstruction</u>, and the development of high-performance software for PDE-constrained problems. Herrmann’s group combines **sparsity-promoting methods**, **randomized sampling**, and *high-performance domain-specific languages* (notably **Devito**) to enable scalable solutions for exploration seismology and emerging applications such as *digital twins for geological carbon storage* and **Bayesian inference** for uncertainty quantification.

---

## Research Areas
Herrmann’s research sits at the intersection of applied mathematics, signal processing, and high-performance scientific computing. He has been a leading proponent of using directional multiscale transforms (notably **curvelet** frames) and sparsity priors to address common seismic-data problems such as missing-trace interpolation, denoising, and primary–multiple separation (see e.g., "Non-parametric seismic data recovery with curvelet frames" (2008), "Seismic denoising with nonuniformly sampled curvelets" (2006), and "Curvelet-based seismic data processing: A multiscale and nonlinear approach" (2008)). Complementing sparsity-based approaches, his work develops randomized and compressive strategies for acquisition and inversion—randomized sampling, source-encoding, and compressive simulation—to reduce computational and acquisition costs ("Randomized sampling and sparsity" (2010); "Fast randomized full-waveform inversion with compressive sensing" (2012); "Designing simultaneous acquisitions with compressive sensing" (2009)). Herrmann has also advanced methods for robust and scalable full-waveform inversion (FWI), addressing nonconvexity and robustness via expanded search spaces, penalty and constraint formulations, and heavy‑tailed noise models ("Mitigating local minima in full-waveform inversion by expanding the search space" (2013); "A penalty method for PDE-constrained optimization in inverse problems" (2016); "Robust full-waveform inversion using the Student’s t-distribution" (2011)). On the software and computational side, he has co-led the development of **Devito**, a domain-specific language for automated high-performance finite-difference solvers, and contributed frameworks for symbolic and scalable implementations of inversion algorithms ("Devito (v3.1.0)..." (2019); "Architecture and performance of Devito" (2020); "A large-scale framework for symbolic implementations of seismic inversion algorithms in Julia" (2019)). More recent directions include uncertainty quantification and Bayesian approaches for PDE-constrained inverse problems, machine‑learning surrogates for large-scale PDEs (e.g., Fourier neural operators), and the concept of *digital twins* for geological carbon storage where accurate, fast forward models and quantified uncertainty are essential ("Uncertainty quantification for inverse problems..." (2018); "Model-parallel Fourier neural operators..." (2023)).

## Notable Works
- "Non-parametric seismic data recovery with curvelet frames" (2008) — seminal work demonstrating how **curvelet-based sparsity promotion** can robustly recover missing seismic traces and improve downstream imaging; established an influential paradigm for curvelet-domain data recovery and interpolation.
- "Devito (v3.1.0): an embedded domain-specific language for finite differences and geophysical exploration" (2019) / "Architecture and performance of Devito" (2020) — creation and engineering of **Devito**, enabling automated, high-performance finite-difference solvers that make large-scale FWI and migration workflows tractable and reproducible.
- "Mitigating local minima in full-waveform inversion by expanding the search space" (2013) — methodological advance in FWI that reduces sensitivity to poor starting models by reformulating the inversion search space, improving convergence in complex geological settings.

## Academic Background
Herrmann has an extensive publication record dating back to the late 1990s and is a highly cited authority in computational seismology (≈11,814 citations across his body of work). Based at Georgia Tech, he holds joint appointments bridging **earth sciences** and **computational science & engineering**, reflecting a career that combines rigorous mathematical modeling, algorithm design, and open-source software development. His leadership is evidenced by major community artifacts (Devito, Sparco) and numerous collaborative contributions on compressive acquisition, PDE-constrained optimization, and uncertainty quantification. Herrmann’s work has been supported by multidisciplinary grants and close partnerships across academia and industry, and he is routinely involved in mentoring graduate students and postdocs who go on to contribute to computational geophysics, numerical PDEs, and data-driven earth-system modeling.

## Overview
Edmond Chow is a professor at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group in **high-performance scientific computing** within the College of Computing. His work centers on the development of scalable algorithms and software for large-scale scientific simulations and numerical linear algebra. He is best known for contributions that bridge *algorithm design* and *high-performance implementation*, with particular emphasis on <u>sparse linear algebra</u>, <u>preconditioning</u>, <u>parallel algorithms</u>, and <u>molecular dynamics simulations</u>. His research agenda addresses both theoretical algorithmic advances and practical optimizations for modern many-core and distributed architectures.

---

## Research Areas
Chow's research spans several interlocking areas in computational science. In *molecular dynamics* and biomolecular simulation he has produced highly cited work on scaling MD codes to commodity clusters ("Scalable algorithms for molecular dynamics simulations on commodity clusters", 2006) and on adapting algorithms to special-purpose machines such as Anton ("Millisecond-scale molecular dynamics simulations on Anton", 2009). In numerical linear algebra and preconditioning he has developed and analyzed a family of approximate inverse and incomplete LU techniques (e.g., "Approximate inverse preconditioners via sparse-sparse iterations", 1998; "Experimental study of ILU preconditioners for indefinite matrices", 1997), including modern parallel and GPU-oriented variants ("ParILUT---A new parallel threshold ILU factorization", 2018; "Asynchronous iterative algorithm for computing incomplete factorizations on GPUs", 2015). He has also addressed low-level kernels and performance engineering—optimizing sparse matrix-vector multiplication on many-core processors ("Efficient sparse matrix-vector multiplication on x86-based many-core processors", 2013) and proposing iterative approaches for sparse triangular solves ("Iterative sparse triangular solves for preconditioning", 2015). Other threads include scalable graph algorithms (distributed BFS on BlueGene/L), parallel multigrid and preconditioner library design (hypre), rank-structured kernel matrix methods (H2Pack, SMASH, proxy point techniques), and applications to quantum chemistry and Brownian dynamics where scalable Fock matrix construction and hydrodynamic interaction computations are central.

---

## Notable Works
- "Scalable algorithms for molecular dynamics simulations on commodity clusters" (2006) — introduced algorithmic and implementation techniques that enabled substantially larger and longer MD simulations on commodity hardware, influencing both software (Desmond) and special-purpose machine work.
- "Approximate inverse preconditioners via sparse-sparse iterations" (1998) and related ILU studies (1997–2003) — provided foundational analysis and practical algorithms for robust preconditioning of general sparse and indefinite systems, spawning many parallel and GPU adaptations.
- "Efficient sparse matrix-vector multiplication on x86-based many-core processors" (2013) and subsequent kernel-focused papers — identified practical bottlenecks and optimizations for SpMV and triangular solves on modern architectures, informing high-performance implementations used in iterative solvers and simulation codes.

---

## Academic Background
Edmond Chow is a senior faculty researcher at Georgia Tech with a long-standing record of interdisciplinary collaboration across academia, national laboratories, and industry (including collaborations with DE Shaw Research and large-scale computing initiatives). His publication portfolio exceeds 11,500 citations, reflecting broad impact in *scientific computing*, *numerical methods*, and *high-performance computing*. He has contributed to major community venues (including SC proceedings and foundational software efforts such as hypre and H2Pack), mentored students and postdocs, and played active roles in advancing scalable solver technology and performance engineering for emerging computer architectures.
